concepts.title: Frame of reference
concepts.conceptBody:
Vector Form of Relative Velocity

Consider two frames of reference \(S\) and \(S^{\prime}\) and suppose the particle \(\mathrm{P}\) is observed from both the frames. The frames may be moving with respect to each other. The position vector of the particle \(\mathrm{P}\) with respect to the frame \(\mathrm{S}\) is \(\overline{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~S}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}\). The position vector of the particle with respect to the frame \(\mathrm{S}^{\prime}\) is \(\overline{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~S}^1}=\overline{\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}\). The position of the frame \(\mathrm{S}^{\prime}\) (the origin of frame \(\mathrm{S}^{\prime}\) in fact) with respect to the frame \(\mathrm{S}\) is \(\mathrm{OO}^{\prime}\).
Now, \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OO}^{\prime}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}}\)

\(\begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{ps}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{ps}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{s}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{ps}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{ps}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{\mathrm{s}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}}
\end{aligned}\)
Differentiate w.r.t. time
\(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{ps}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{ps}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{s}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}}\)
Differentiate again
\(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{ps}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{ps}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{\mathrm{s}^{\prime} \mathrm{s}}\)
In general
\(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{12}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} 1 \mathrm{~s}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} 2 \mathrm{~s}\)
or \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_1=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{12}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_2\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text{or } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{12}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_1-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_2 \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{A}_1 \mathrm{D}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{BC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{CD}} \quad \ldots (3)
\end{aligned}\)
Similarly in cartesian form
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_1(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{V}_1, 2(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{x}) \\
& \mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{y})=\mathrm{V}_{1,2}(\mathrm{y})+\mathrm{V}_2(\mathrm{y})
\end{aligned}\)
Also, \(\frac{\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_2}{\mathrm{dt}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{12}, \frac{\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{12}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{12}\)

concepts.title: Shortest Distance of approach
concepts.conceptBody:
Shortest Distance of approach

Example

I starts from origin in $x$ direction with $v=40\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ km}/\text{hr}2$ is initially $20\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ km}$ from origin and starts in $y$ direction as shown in fig. Find out shortest distance and time takes to reach.

Solution. 

${{\vec{V}}_{12}}={{\vec{V}}_{1}}-{{\vec{V}}_{2}}=40\tilde{i}-\left( 30\tilde{j} \right) =\left( 40\tilde{i}-30\tilde{j} \right)$

$\text{tan}\phi =\left| \frac{{{{\vec{v}}}_{1}}}{{{{\vec{v}}}_{2}}} \right|=\frac{30}{40}=\frac{3}{4},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\phi ={{37}^{\circ }}$

$\left| {{{\vec{V}}}_{12}} \right|=\sqrt{{{40}^{2}}+{{30}^{2}}}=50\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ km}/\text{hr}$

Now, once we have studied the motion of 1 with respect to 2, we can consider 2, to be stationary and then

Analyze the motion as if we are sitting on 2

\(\therefore time=1\frac{\sin \theta }{{{{\vec{v}}}_{12}}}=\left( \frac{12}{50} \right)=0.245\)

2nd Method: (Not preferable)

Perform the analysis from ground

\(S=\sqrt{{{({{x}_{2}}-{{x}_{1}})}^{2}}+{{({{y}_{2}}+{{y}_{1}})}^{2}}+{{({{z}_{2}}-{{z}_{1}})}^{2}}}\) and put \(\left( \frac{ds}{dt} \right)=0\)

concepts.title: River Boat Problems
concepts.conceptBody:
River Boat Problems

In river boat problems we come across the following three terms :

${{\vec{v}}_{\text{r}}}=$ absolute velocity of river

${{\vec{v}}_{\text{br}}}=$ velocity of boatman with respect to river or velocity of boatman in still water

and ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{b}}}=$ absolute velocity of boatman

Here, it is important to note that ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{br}}}$ is the velocity of boatman with which he steers and ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{b}}}$ is the actual velocity of boatman relative to ground.

Further, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\vec{v}}_{\text{b}}}={{\vec{v}}_{\text{br}}}={{\vec{v}}_{\text{r}}}$

Now, let us derive some standard results and their special cases.

A boatman starts from point A on one bank of a river with velocity ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{br}}}$ in the direction shown in Fig. River is flowing along positive $\text{x}$-direction with velocity ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{r}}}$. Width of the river is w. Then

${{\vec{v}}_{\text{b}}}={{\vec{v}}_{\text{r}}}+{{\vec{v}}_{\text{br}}}$

Therefore, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{v}}_{\text{bx}}}={{\text{v}}_{\text{rx}}}+{{\text{v}}_{\text{brx}}}={{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}-{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{sin}\theta $

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{v}}_{\text{by}}}={{\text{v}}_{\text{ry}}}+{{\text{v}}_{\text{bry}}}=0+{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{cos}\theta ={{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{cos}\theta $

Now, time taken by the boatman to cross the river is :

$\text{t}=\frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{by}}}}=\frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{cos}\theta }$ … (i)

Further, displacement along x-axis when he reaches on the other bank (also called drift) is

$\text{x}={{\text{v}}_{\text{bx}}}\text{t}=\left( {{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}-{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{sin}\theta \right)\frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{cos}\theta }$… (ii)

concepts.title: Special Conditions in river Boat Problems
concepts.conceptBody:
Special Cases

(i) Condition when the boatman crosses the river in shortest interval of time 

From equation (i) we can see that time (t) will be minimum when $\theta =0$, i.e. the boatman should steer his boat perpendicular to the river current.

Also, ${{\text{t}}_{\text{min}}}=\frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cos}\theta =1$

(ii) Condition when the boatman wants to reach point B, i.e., at a point just opposite from where he started 

In this case, the drift $\left( \text{x} \right)$ should be zero

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=0$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( {{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}-{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{sin}\theta \right)\frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{cos}\theta }=0$ or ${{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}={{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{sin}\theta $

or $\text{sin}\theta =\frac{{{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}}$ or $\theta =\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{{{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}} \right)$

Hence, to reach point $B$ the boatman should row at an angle $\theta =\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{{{v}_{r}}}{{{v}_{br}}} \right)$ upstream from AB. Further, since $\text{sin}\theta >1$

So, if ${{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}\ge {{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}$, the boatman can never reach at point B. Because if ${{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}\ge {{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}},\text{sin}\theta =90$ and it is just impossible to reach at $B$ if $\theta =90$. Moreover it can be seen that ${{v}_{b}}=0$ if ${{v}_{r}}={{v}_{br}}$ and $\theta $ $=90$. Similarly, if ${{v}_{r}}>{{v}_{br}}$, $\text{sin}\theta >1$, i.e., no such angle exists. Practically it can be realized in this manner that it is not possible to reach at $\text{B}$ if river velocity $\left( {{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}} \right)$ is too high.

(iii) Shortest path

Path length travelled by the boatman when he reaches the opposite shore is

$\text{s}=\sqrt{{{\text{w}}^{2}}+{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$

When ${{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}<{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}$. In this case $\text{x}=0$, when $\theta =\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{{{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}} \right)$

or $ $

When ${{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}>{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}$ : In this case $\text{x}$ is minimum, where $\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{d}\theta }=0$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}\theta }\left\{ \frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}-\text{cos}\theta }\left( {{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}-{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{sin}\theta \right) \right\}=0 \\
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & -{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta -\left( {{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}-{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}\text{sin}\theta \right)\left( -\text{sin}\theta \right)=0 \\
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & -{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}+{{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}\text{sin}\theta =0 \\
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \theta =\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{{{\text{v}}_{\text{br}}}}{{{\text{v}}_{\text{r}}}} \right) \\
\end{array}$

Now, at this angle we can find ${{\text{x}}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ and then ${{\text{s}}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$

concepts.title: Rain Man Problems
concepts.conceptBody:
Rain Problems

In these type of problems we again come across three terms ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{r}}},{{\vec{v}}_{\text{m}}}$ and ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{rm}}}$. Here 
$\vec{v}=$ velocity of rain

${{\vec{v}}_{\text{r}}}=$ velocity of man (it may be velocity of cyclist or velocity of motorist also)

and ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{m}}}=$ velocity of rain with respect to man

Here ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{rm}}}$ is the velocity of rain which appears to the man.

concepts.title: Velocity of Approach
concepts.conceptBody:
Velocity of Approach

$\frac{-\text{ds}}{\text{dt}}=\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{rear }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{front }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)$ along the line joining the particles.

Velocity of approach can be found by taking the component of relative velocity along the line joining the particles.

While deciding the front and rear object, the direction of arrow of the line joining the particles can be taken as a guide.

Velocity of approach $=\left( \frac{-\text{ds}}{\text{dt}} \right)={{\vec{V}}_{\text{rear }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}-{{\vec{V}}_{\text{front }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$

$\left( \frac{-\text{ds}}{\text{dt}} \right)={{\text{V}}_{1}}\text{cos}{{\theta }_{1}}-\left( {{\text{V}}_{2}}\text{cos}{{\theta }_{2}} \right)$

Velocity of separation $={{\text{V}}_{\text{front }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{rear }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$

$\frac{+\text{ds}}{\text{dt}}=\left( {{\text{V}}_{2}}\text{cos}{{\theta }_{2}}-{{\text{V}}_{1}}\text{cos}{{\theta }_{1}} \right)$

A boat is moving with velocity of $3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}$ in river and water is moving with a velocity of $-3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}$ with respect to ground. Relative velocity of boat with respect to water is :

The distance between two particles is decreasing at the rate of $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. If these particles travel with same speeds and in the same direction, then the separation increase at the rate of $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$. The particles have speeds as

A river is flowing from $\mathrm{W}$ to $\mathrm{E}$ with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}$. A man can swim in still water with a velocity $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}$. In which direction should the man swim so as to take the shortest possible path to go to the south
